SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's central empirical claims (brute-force ranking outperforms first-/second-order approximations, and iterative re-ranking improves results) are directly evaluated via experiments on the MNIST subset and a toy regression task, so they are plausibly supported. However, important technical details are missing or deferred (full second-order derivative derivations in supplementary material, no code or hyperparameters reported), the experimental scope is limited (only a 5k-image MNIST subset and a synthetic task), and no statistical tests or broader baselines are reported. These omissions raise concerns about reproducibility and the generality of the empirical conclusions, so the work is not without technical reservations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a focused, empirically grounded perspective—systematically comparing brute-force (oracle) neuron removal to first- and second-order Taylor approximations and proposing iterative re-ranking for pruning without any retraining—which is a concrete methodological contribution not merely a rehash of standard pruning heuristics. However, the underlying problem (neuron pruning/model compression) is well-studied and the techniques are largely comparative and approximation-based rather than a fundamentally new theoretical framework; experiments are also limited to MNIST subset and a toy task. Given these facts, the contribution is a moderate, incremental novelty that clearly differentiates its specific comparisons and insights (e.g., non-uniform ‘‘elite’’ neurons, tradeoffs between cost and pruning quality) but is not highly original.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro, method description, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early and followed through in the methodology and empirical sections. Notation and presentation are generally clear and writing quality is good, with adequate related-work coverage and informative reporting of datasets and evaluation metrics. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity: key derivations and implementation details are deferred to the supplement, and hyperparameters/code availability are not reported, which makes parts of the experimental setup harder to assess.

main_claims: Introduces and evaluates a novel algorithmic approach for pruning entire neurons from trained neural networks without any retraining, using brute-force, first-order (linear) and second-order (quadratic) Taylor-based ranking criteria.

Shows that brute-force (oracle) neuron-removal ranking outperforms first- and second-order approximations in identifying removable neurons without retraining, and that iterative re-ranking (greedy update of rankings after each removal) improves results over a single overall ranking.

Provides empirical evidence that learned representations are highly non-uniform across hidden units (a few ‘‘elite’’ neurons vs. many noise-canceling units), and that substantial fractions of neurons (reported 40–60% for some networks, up to much higher in toy cases) can be removed with negligible effect on performance.

Demonstrates that second-order Taylor approximations generally perform better than first-order approximations but still fall short of brute-force, and highlights trade-offs between computational cost and pruning quality.
_summary.methodology: For a pre-trained network, compute per-neuron estimates of the change in total error under removal using (a) brute-force forward passes turning each neuron off, (b) a first-order Taylor approximation using neuron outputs and first derivatives, and (c) a second-order Taylor approximation including second derivatives; produce neuron rankings and remove neurons either via a single overall ranking or an iterative re-ranking (recomputing rankings after each removal), all without any retraining.
metrics: Total error / change in total error
limitations: Brute-force ranking is computationally expensive (impractical for very large networks), which motivated use of a 5000-image MNIST subset and input downsampling in experiments.
Single overall ranking algorithm is intentionally naive and performs poorly for deeper networks; iterative re-ranking is more costly but more effective.

Taylor-series based approximations (especially first-order) can make poor pruning decisions and are inconsistent compared to brute-force; second-order terms are more difficult to compute and may require thresholding heuristics.

Experiments evaluate pruning without any retraining, so results characterize pruning decisions absent recovery via further training (authors note retraining would conflate pruning quality with network fault-recovery).
Some derivations and implementation details (e.g., full second-order derivative computations) are deferred to supplementary material.
_summary.topic_area: ML - neural network pruning / model compression
